metadata.title: DC AND RF TECHNIQUES FOR COMPUTING THE SERIES RESISTANCE OF THE EQUIVALENT ELECTRICAL CIRCUIT FOR SEMICONDUCTOR LASERS
metadata.abstract: **Horacio L. Martinez-Reyes,[1] J. Apolinar Reynoso-Hernandez,[1] and F. Javier Mendieta[1]** 1 ´ ´ ´ CICESE � Centro de Investigacion Cientıfica y de Educacion SuperiorEnsenada,deB.C., MexicoEnsenada´ 22800
metadata.introduction:
The intrinsic equivalent circuit of a laser diode, for currents below threshold, can be reduced to a simple _RC_ circuit as shown in Figure 1. The extrinsic equivalent circuit depends on parasitics of the device, and is modeled with a parasitic series resistance _RS_ introduced by contacts and cladding layers, and a bonding wire inductance _L_ , as shown in Figure 2 ��1 . 

Laser diodes with intrinsic modulation bandwidths above 40 GHz have been demonstrated. However, the maximum measured bandwidths reported to date �2�4� are notably lower than the intrinsic modulation bandwidths of these devices. This disagreement is usually attributed to electrical parasitics and on a minor scale to device heating ��5 , and storage charge capacitance ��6 . 

As we can see, the parasitics play an important role in frequency response; however, usually, extrinsic elements of the equivalent electrical circuit of semiconductor lasers are

evidence.original_text: The series resistance of the equivalent circuit for laser diodes is computed by dc and RF techniques used in diodes and transistors for microwaves.
The series resistance of the equivalent circuit for laser diodes is computed by dc and RF techniques used in diodes and transistors for microwaves.
The series resistance of the equivalent circuit for laser diodes is computed by dc and RF techniques used in diodes and transistors for microwaves.
The methods are in good agreement with measurements in a DFB laser diode.

Bennet’s method consists of the determination of the minimum of the function X(RS, a, b) defined as where a = nUT and b = -a ln(IS). The minimum of X occurs for m measurements of I versus VD when the partial derivatives with respect to RS, a, and b are equal to zero.

In Cheung and Cheung’s method, Eq. (2) is differentiated with respect to ln I and, after restructuring terms, the following equation is obtained: This equation describes the linear behavior of d(VD)/d(ln I) versus the current I, where RS is the slope.
The first RF method uses the Z11-parameter calculated from the parameter S11 measured for a current I below threshold.
For the first RF method, the measurement of the S11-parameter was made in the range of 45–300 MHz with an HP8510C network analyzer for a current below threshold (I = 6 mA).
For the first RF method, the measurement of the S11-parameter was made in the range of 45–300 MHz with an HP8510C network analyzer for a current below threshold (I = 6 mA).

We made the fitting to an equivalent electrical circuit for a current below threshold (I = 6 mA), with the microwave systems simulator "ACADEMY", with the circuit shown in Figures 4 and 5, where the series resistance is 5.60 Ω.

Bennet’s method consists of the determination of the minimum of the function X(RS, a, b) defined as where a = nUT and b = -a ln(IS). The minimum of X occurs for m measurements of I versus VD when the partial derivatives with respect to RS, a, and b are equal to zero.

In Cheung and Cheung’s method, Eq. (2) is differentiated with respect to ln I and, after restructuring terms, the following equation is obtained: This equation describes the linear behavior of d(VD)/d(ln I) versus the current I, where RS is the slope.
In this paper, we present and compare two dc methods and three RF methods used in microwave diodes and transistors applied to the computation of the parasitic series resistance RS.
In this paper, we present and compare two dc methods and three RF methods used in microwave diodes and transistors applied to the computation of the parasitic series resistance RS.
These conditions lead to the resolution of three simultaneous linear equations, and the variables RS, a, and b are obtained using a least squares optimization method.

Measurements of the series resistance were performed on a DFB semiconductor laser type FLD150F2RH from Fujitsu. With a threshold current Ith = 17 mA at T = 293 K, the two dc methods were developed with 40 measurements from 4 to 8 mA.

entries.task: measurement of series resistance on DFB laser

evidence.original_text: For the first RF method, the measurement of the S11-parameter was made in the range of 45–300 MHz with an HP8510C network analyzer for a current below threshold (I = 6 mA). In the second RF method, we measured the S11-parameter with the network analyzer for currents below threshold (I = 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, and 12 mA).
In Bennet’s method, Eq. (3) was applied, and Cheung and Cheung’s method (4) was applied. In both cases, a series resistance of 5.59 Ω was obtained as is shown in Table 1.

In the first RF method, we can see from Eq. (5) that the series resistance RS is the value of the real part of the laser impedance at high frequencies, and at low frequencies, the real part is RS + Rd. Thus, from the response in Figure 4, the real part of laser diode impedance is Re(Z) = 7.5 Ω for a frequency f = 300 MHz; in this way, by evaluating the real part of Eq. (5) with the fitting values of Rd and C at 300 MHz, we obtain Re(Z) at 300 MHz = RS + Rd/(1 + (ωRdC)^2) = RS + 1.86 Ω. So the series resistance is RS = Re(Z) - 1.86 Ω = 5.64 Ω, as shown in Table 1.

In the second RF method, we took the values of Re(Z) at frequencies below threshold (5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, and 12 mA), and we plotted Re(Z) at f=0 versus 1/I as is shown in Figure 6. The value of RS was determined with the fitting in Figure 6, adjusting the measurement values in a straight line, and taking the value of Re(Z) for 1/I = 0, as indicated in Eq. (8). The value obtained for RS is 5.32 Ω, as is shown in Table 1.

In the third RF method, in Figure 7, RS was obtained at low frequencies according to Eq. (9). The plot in Figure 7 is obtained at a current above threshold I = 25 mA. In this method, the series resistance RS = 6.21 Ω as shown in Table 1.

We made the fitting to an equivalent electrical circuit for a current below threshold (I = 6 mA), with the microwave systems simulator "ACADEMY", with the circuit shown in Figures 4 and 5, where the series resistance is 5.60 Ω, as shown in Table 1.

The extrinsic equivalent circuit of a laser diode, which takes into account the parasitic series resistance introduced by contacts and cladding layers, plays an important role in frequency response. In this paper, we compute the series resistance of a DFB laser with dc and RF techniques used in microwaves diodes and transistors modeling.
As we can see in Table 1, dc and RF methods and fitting for a DFB laser are in good agreement. The series resistance computed with both dc methods and the value from fitting is RS = 5.59 Ω.
The fitting quality at frequencies lower than 300 MHz was very good, as shown in Figures 4 and 5. At higher frequencies, a model with lumped circuit elements is no longer valid.

We could not get the lowest response expected at frequencies above 300 MHz for structure effects in the laser diode, and in this manner, we evaluate Eq. (5) for computing the real value of the series resistance.

The third method, which takes the real part of the impedance Re(Z) at low frequencies (f = 45 MHz) for a current above threshold, exhibited the greatest variation in the series resistance value among all methods presented in this paper.
However, we estimate that we can reduce this variation by performing measurements below 45 MHz.
